data.title: Home BancShares outlines Mountain Commerce conversion in November and targets end of 2026 for maximum cost saves
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Home BancShares (HOMB) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “We’re happy to have completed the merger with our acquisition of Mountain Commerce… we will not be able to start converting Mountain Commerce until November. As a result, the maximum anticipated savings will not be realized until probably the end of ’26.” (Chairman & CEO John Allison)
  • “We had a… $110 million Texas credit that we decided to non-perform this quarter… We do not anticipate any additional loss… and do not expect this loan to have any major impact on earnings, if any, at all.” (Chairman & CEO Allison)
  • “We continue to repurchase stock… If the volatility continues, we will be very active on the repurchase side… I want to buy that back… It’s about 5.5 million shares.” (Chairman & CEO Allison)
  • “During Q1, we grew the portfolio to approximately $2.1 billion… Private Credit balances peaked at just under $500 million at the end of 2022. And today, outstanding are $87 million… a reduction of over 80% in the past 3 years.” (President of Centennial Commercial Finance Group Christopher Poulton)
  • “The first quarter of 2026 was a good start to the year with $118.2 million in net income… The reported Net Interest Margin was 4.51%… Deposit balances increased $258 million… we repurchased 507,000 shares of stock during the quarter for a total of $13.9 million.” (Chief Operating Officer John Tipton)
  • “I’m pleased with the quarter. there’s not really any noise to it, so it’s just kind of solid Core Earnings.” (CFO Brian Davis)

Outlook

  • “We do expect slightly higher payoffs in Q2… our pipeline should allow us to replace those balances either this quarter or the next.” (President Poulton)
  • “I see this downward trend continuing in the Legacy Bank into the second quarter because Q2 and Q3 projected payoffs are very high. The MCB acquisition will, however, add over $1.49 billion in loans to the balance sheet.” (President & Chief Lending Officer Kevin Hester)
  • “Deposit balances increased $258 million… I would expect some headwinds in Q2 from tax payments.” (Chief Operating Officer Tipton)
  • No formal EPS or revenue guidance was provided in the prepared remarks or Q&A.

Financial Results

  • “$118.2 million in net income, a 2.09% Return on Assets, and 16.56% Return on Tangible Common Equity.” (Chief Operating Officer Tipton)
  • “The reported Net Interest Margin was 4.51%, down 10 basis points from Q4, as there was zero Event income in Q1.” (Chief Operating Officer Tipton)
  • “Noninterest-bearing balances grew by $126 million to almost $4 billion and now account for 22.5% of total deposits.” (Chief Operating Officer Tipton)
  • “Core expenses were about $115 million for the quarter… Mountain Commerce probably adds $7 million, $7.5 million a quarter to that number right now… the majority will come middle of fourth quarter.” (Chief Operating Officer Tipton)

Q&A

  • Stephen Scouten, Piper Sandler: asked if HOMB would loosen “the triple-accretive mantra” to get a deal done; Chairman & CEO Allison: “We hold pretty tight to our philosophy… I’m the largest individual shareholder, and I’m not interested in diluting myself.”
  • Stephen Scouten, Piper Sandler: asked about loan-yield move and the Texas nonaccrual impact; COO Tipton: “It was about $1.6 million impact for the quarter… about 4 basis points to NIM… production yields… 7.20% to 7.25% for the first quarter.”
  • David Rochester, Cantor Fitzgerald: asked about organic loan trend and payoff sources; President & CLO Hester: “It feels a little soft second quarter… we’re going to have to get the production in here and get it on the books.”
  • David Rochester, Cantor Fitzgerald: asked about merger margin impact; COO Tipton: “We’re still in the process of finalizing the purchase-accounting marks… expect a little pressure at least initially on the margin.”
  • Brett Rabatin, Hovde: asked about expense growth and Mountain Commerce “noise”; COO Tipton: “Mountain Commerce probably adds $7 million, $7.5 million a quarter… until… their conversion… majority… middle of fourth quarter.”
  • Catherine Mealor, KBW: asked about deposit-cost trajectory; COO Tipton: “I’m still optimistic that we can inch out a basis point or two… but… we’re seeing banks offer 4% for CDs.”
  • Michael Rose, Raymond James: asked to quantify scheduled payoffs; President & CLO Hester: “Second quarter looks like close to $1 billion and third quarter could approach that.”
  • Matt Olney, Stephens: asked about private credit outlook and when opportunity returns; President Poulton: “It feels early… price doesn’t fix credit… we’d want to see a little more capitulation… before we would do that.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative analyst tone, concentrated on loan growth visibility, margin sensitivity, deposit-cost competition, and credit-outlier containment, including “how aggressive would you be” on M&A and whether to “loosen” deal standards. (Multiple analysts)
  • Slightly positive management tone in prepared remarks, with defensive clarity on dilution and credit containment: “We do not anticipate any additional loss” and “I’m not interested in diluting myself.” (Chairman & CEO Allison)
  • Compared with Q4, the current call carried more credit- and payoff-specific scrutiny, while Q4 included broader industry commentary on “dilution, dilution, dilution” and the Mountain Commerce LOI rather than post-close integration details. (Chairman & CEO Allison)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 shifted from LOI to integration timing: Q4 said “we’re excited about our announced LOI with Mountain Commerce,” while Q1 said conversion cannot start “until November” and “maximum anticipated savings” comes “end of ’26.” (Chairman & CEO Allison)
  • Margin and loan-yield discussion pivoted from Q4 core margin framing to Q1’s “zero Event income” and an explicit nonaccrual interest reversal quantified at “$1.6 million.” (Chief Operating Officer Tipton)
  • Analysts moved from Q4 questions on growth drivers and deposit funding capacity to Q1’s heavier focus on payoff quantification (“close to $1 billion”) and merger-related NIM pressure. (Analysts; President & CLO Hester; COO Tipton)

Risks and Concerns

  • “Inflation is not dead… The question is how high and how long do they remain high.” (Chairman & CEO Allison)
  • “We’re being very careful on the loan side because of the uncertainty… asset class, and what this cycle may ultimately evolve into.” (Chairman & CEO Allison)
  • “There is some rate pressure… even some underwriting and structure pressure… that people have given into.” (President & CLO Hester)

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as a steady quarter with “solid Core Earnings,” high capital, and active buybacks, while placing investor attention on three near-term swing factors: the timing and magnitude of Mountain Commerce cost saves (conversion “until November,” with “maximum anticipated savings” by “end of ’26”), elevated scheduled payoffs (“close to $1 billion” in both Q2 and Q3), and the resolution path for the $110 million Texas credit now on nonaccrual, which leadership said is expected to have “no major impact on earnings, if any, at all.”

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Alcoa anticipates $135M 2026 interest expense while environmental and ARO payments rise to about $360M
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Alcoa Corporation (AA) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We had a strong start to 2026, driven by execution, and we are well positioned to deliver a strong second quarter and full year 2026 performance," said William Oplinger (President, CEO & Director), while also pointing to continuity of supply despite "significant disruption in the Middle East" and saying, "Our flexible casthouse network continues to unlock value-add opportunities."

  • William Oplinger (President, CEO & Director) highlighted strategic items and balance-sheet actions: "We continue to anticipate ministerial approvals by year-end 2026," "We are in advanced discussions on the monetization of our former Massena East smelter site for a data center project," and "On April 7, we successfully and safely completed the restart of the San Ciprian smelter." He also said the company "issued notice to redeem the remaining $219 million outstanding of our 2028 notes," citing "our strong cash balance of $1.4 billion."

  • "Revenue decreased 7% sequentially to $3.2 billion," said Molly Beerman (Executive VP & CFO), and added that first-quarter results included "net income attributable to Alcoa" of "$425 million" and "earnings per common share" of "$1.60 per share." She also reported that "Adjusted EBITDA was $595 million" and said inventory repositioning "deferred EBITDA recognition on 30,000 metric tons to the second quarter."

Outlook

  • "We have 2 updates to our 2026 full year outlook," Molly Beerman (Executive VP & CFO) said: "Interest expense will decrease slightly to $135 million" and "our estimate for environmental and ARO payments has increased to approximately $360 million, up from $325 million."

  • For Q2, Molly Beerman (Executive VP & CFO) said the Alumina segment is expected to be "unfavorable by approximately $15 million" and the Aluminum segment "favorable by $55 million," while adding, "We expect tariff costs to increase by approximately $35 million" and "we expect the second quarter of 2026 operational tax expense to approximate $110 million to $120 million."

  • Compared with Q4 2025 guidance language, the company moved from Q1 headwinds tied to "the non-recurrence of Spain and Norway CO2 compensation" (Q4 call) to Q2 benefit drivers including "higher shipments and product premiums" and "the completion of the San Ciprian smelter restart" (Q1 call).

Financial Results

  • Molly Beerman (Executive VP & CFO) reported first-quarter profitability and key bridges: "First quarter net income attributable to Alcoa was $425 million versus the prior quarter of $213 million," "net special items of $52 million," and "a mark-to-market gain of $88 million on the Ma'aden shares."

  • On cash and leverage, Molly Beerman (Executive VP & CFO) said, "We finished the quarter with a cash balance of $1.4 billion and adjusted net debt of $1.8 billion," and added, "Free cash flow was negative $298 million for the quarter, primarily reflecting seasonal working capital build."

  • On segment-level cadence, Molly Beerman (Executive VP & CFO) said a North America inventory repositioning "deferring revenue recognition until the second quarter" was intended to "provid[e] casthouse flexibility for additional value-add product production and shipments, which yield higher margins."

Q&A

  • Carlos de Alba, Morgan Stanley: "what is the impact of the Middle East smelters reducing operating rate for Alcoa's alumina shipments"; William Oplinger (President, CEO & Director): "We are working with our customers to redirect those shipments" and "no direct impacts from profitability" beyond "API pricing."

  • William Peterson, JPMorgan: "unpack" the "$15 million" Alumina Q2 headwind and raw-material exposure; Molly Beerman (Executive VP & CFO): "Lower pricing volumes from bauxite offtake agreements represent $10 million" and energy is "primarily diesel," adding, "we do not have concerns at this point on supply."

  • Katja Jancic, BMO: whether San Ciprian is profitable; Molly Beerman (Executive VP & CFO): "The smelter is doing very well" but "we're continuing to have significant losses at the refinery" and "the refinery remains very challenged."

  • Nick Giles, B. Riley: whether conflict changes capital allocation and update on idle sites; William Oplinger (President, CEO & Director): "the conflict hasn't changed our capital allocation framework" and on sequencing, "You should not assume that the highest value will be monetized first."

  • Nick Giles, B. Riley: Warrick restart requirements; William Oplinger (President, CEO & Director): "it will require about $100 million of capital" and "we think it will be 1 to 2 years for that restart."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly negative to pressing on redirection risk, input-cost inflation, tariffs, and restart economics, reflected in questions like "Any impact on profitability or margins" (Morgan Stanley) and "do the operations...are they profitable" (BMO).

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more explanatory in Q&A, with confidence language including "we are well positioned" (President, CEO & Director Oplinger) and "we do not have concerns at this point on supply" (Executive VP & CFO Beerman), alongside caveats such as "may not recur" on currency impacts (Executive VP & CFO Beerman).

  • Versus Q4 2025, Q1 2026 placed heavier emphasis on the Middle East disruption as an active driver of premiums, logistics, and cost volatility, while Q4 2025 focused more on CBAM/tariffs context and the in-progress San Ciprian ramp.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The Q1 2026 call repeatedly tied market conditions to "the Middle East conflict" and said "recently exceeding $3,600 per metric ton" on LME (President, CEO & Director Oplinger), while Q4 2025 highlighted CBAM and said LME "recently reached $3,200 per metric ton" (President, CEO & Director Oplinger).

  • Capital allocation shifted from Q4 2025 emphasis on staying within the $1 billion to $1.5 billion net debt range (Executive VP & CFO Beerman) to a Q1 2026 action: "issued notice to redeem" "$219 million" of 2028 notes (President, CEO & Director Oplinger).

  • Analysts in Q1 2026 concentrated on shipment redirection, diesel/caustic/freight lags, and value-add premiums, while Q4 2025 analyst focus leaned more toward tariffs/CBAM mechanics, restart timelines, and monetization process detail.

Risks and Concerns

  • William Oplinger (President, CEO & Director) warned that "disruption in the Middle East" has included "the closure of the Strait of Hormuz" and said "more than 2.5 million tons of annual smelting capacity" are "off-line year-to-date," adding that dependencies "tighten[] supply" and raise "cost volatility."

  • Molly Beerman (Executive VP & CFO) flagged cost and policy pressure points, including diesel exposure in mining, tariff impacts where "Section 232 tariff costs" are expected to rise "approximately $35 million," and higher remediation-related cash needs behind the increase to "approximately $360 million" in environmental and ARO payments.

Final Takeaway

Alcoa management described Q1 2026 as a strong start supported by higher aluminum pricing and operational execution, while emphasizing that the Middle East conflict is reshaping logistics, input costs, and regional premiums. The company kept its full-year volume stance while detailing Q2 drivers: a modest Alumina headwind, a larger Aluminum benefit tied to inventory repositioning, higher shipments and premiums, and the completion of the San Ciprian restart. Management also highlighted balance-sheet actions (redeeming $219 million of 2028 notes), updated full-year interest expense to $135 million, and raised expected environmental and ARO payments to about $360 million.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Oil futures rise as U.S. tightens Iran blockade, Hormuz still shut
data.description:
Crude oil futures gained Thursday after the U.S. said it was expanding its blockade of Iran to "all ships, regardless of nationality" and will pursue Iran-linked ships beyond the Middle East if necessary, while continuing the blockade as long as necessary.

But President Trump announced that the leaders of Israel and Lebanon reached a 10-day ceasefire deal after weeks of fighting between Israel and Iran-backed Hezbollah and said prospects for a deal with Iran are "looking very good," with the two sides in discussions to extend a ceasefire ahead of its expiration next week.

"The market is balancing improving headlines with the reality that every delay in normalization means further supply loss and tighter near-term fundamentals," CIBC Private Wealth Group senior energy trader Rebecca Babin said in a note. "While there's been some positive geopolitical progress, it hasn't translated into real movement on flows, which remain clearly constrained."

Given the damage to energy infrastructure in the Middle East, International Energy Agency head Fatih Birol warned that Europe has "maybe six weeks or so" of jet fuel remaining, and it could take up to two years to recover a significant share of oil and gas production that has been disrupted.

Analysts from ING estimate that ~13M bbl/day of oil flow has been ‌disrupted by ⁠the closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for May delivery jumped 3.7% to $94.69/bbl, and front-month Brent crude (CO1:COM) for June delivery climbed 4.7% to $99.39/bbl.

U.S. natural gas futures (NG1:COM) rose modestly as the market paid little attention to an above-average 59 Bcf storage build that increased the inventory surplus over the five-year average; the front-month Nymex May contract closed up 1.4% at $2.647/MMBtu.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLE)

data.title: Great Southern outlines IT projects adding $200k-$250k a month over next 3-6 quarters
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Great Southern Bancorp (GSBC) Q1 2026

Management View

  • President, CEO & Director Joseph Turner said the quarter was “a solid start to the year in a continuing competitive operating environment,” adding that “both credit and earnings metrics remain strong,” supporting “meaningful per share tangible book value growth,” driven by “disciplined expense management, careful balance sheet structuring and a continued emphasis on relationship-based banking.”
  • President, CEO & Director Turner reported net income of $17.5 million and net interest income of $48.3 million, while describing “a resilient net interest margin” and “stable loan balances.” He attributed lower net interest income versus last year mainly to “the absence of the income from our now terminated interest rate swap,” and said results also reflected “thoughtful capital allocation.”
  • Treasurer Rex Copeland said, “we have recorded approximately $483,000 of additional interest income related to collection of unbooked interest on 3 separate relationships,” and cautioned that “these types of cash basis interest recoveries can occur sporadically.”

Outlook

  • Management did not provide formal earnings or revenue guidance; President, CEO & Director Turner said, “this is one of the reasons... that we don't give guidance is just very difficult to predict,” referring to loan payoffs as the key swing factor.
  • On expenses, President, CEO & Director Turner said IT-related initiatives “will probably happen over the next 3 to 6 quarters” and “could add $200,000 to $250,000 a month to our expense levels,” tied to “data security,” “customer-facing technologies,” and “substantial upgrade in our systems.”
  • On margin sensitivity, Treasurer Copeland said the balance sheet is “pretty balanced” and that a 25-basis-point rate cut “shouldn't be that impactful,” adding, “we don't really anticipate that is going to... negatively impact us very much or for very long.”

Financial Results

  • The company reported net income of $17.5 million, or $1.58 per diluted common share, and an annualized net interest margin of 3.71%.
  • Treasurer Copeland reported total assets of approximately $5.69 billion and total deposits of approximately $4.45 billion at quarter-end; he also reported total net loans (excluding mortgage loans held for sale) increased approximately $99.8 million to $4.46 billion.
  • Credit metrics were described as strong; Treasurer Copeland reported nonperforming assets of approximately $10.1 million, or roughly 0.18% of total assets, and said the company recorded “a negative provision on unfunded commitments of approximately $931,000,” driven by “the decline in unfunded commitments, primarily in unfunded construction balances.”
  • Capital actions were a material theme: Treasurer Copeland said the company repurchased 268,664 shares at an average price of approximately $62.55, declared a quarterly cash dividend of $0.43 per share, and had approximately 419,000 shares remaining under the repurchase authorization.

Q&A

  • Damon Del Monte, Keefe, Bruyette, & Woods: asked for expense run-rate guidance as projects begin; Treasurer Copeland said items reducing Q1 expenses “we don't anticipate those are going to repeat in Q2,” and President, CEO & Director Turner detailed IT projects that “could add $200,000 to $250,000 a month” over “the next 3 to 6 quarters.”
  • Damon Del Monte, Keefe, Bruyette, & Woods: asked about core margin and positioning if a rate cut occurs; Treasurer Copeland said the bank is “pretty well matched” and that a 25-basis-point move “probably isn't going to move the needle too much.”
  • Damon Del Monte, Keefe, Bruyette, & Woods: asked about visibility into paydowns and sustained loan growth; President, CEO & Director Turner said, “it's too volatile to really give guidance, and that's why we choose not to.”
  • John Rodis, Brean Capital: confirmed whether the $200,000 to $250,000 was monthly; President, CEO & Director Turner replied, “Yes. Yes,” and later agreed that the quarterly expense level thinking was reasonable: “I think you are, yes.”
  • John Rodis, Brean Capital: asked if the company is “still a buyer” of stock; President, CEO & Director Turner said, “we do still think our stock at an attractive level,” and Treasurer Copeland added repurchase pace depends on “our total capital” and “continued loan growth.”
  • John Rodis, Brean Capital: asked about higher annuity commissions; Treasurer Copeland said annuity-related levels have been higher for “2, 3, 4 quarters,” and President, CEO & Director Turner said it is “sort of an alternative to CDs.”
  • John Rodis, Brean Capital: asked about securities portfolio trajectory; Treasurer Copeland said it will “go down kind of slowly” with “probably not much in the way of added to the portfolio.”
  • John Rodis, Brean Capital: asked about branch actions after a St. Louis location update; President, CEO & Director Turner said the bank is “always looking at customer patterns,” while Treasurer Copeland said the site will use “ITMs” and “there just won't be an inside lobby present.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was neutral-to-slightly negative, centering on expense step-up, margin durability under rate moves, and uncertainty in loan paydowns (e.g., Del Monte asked to “help us quantify” expenses and sought “visibility into expected pace of pay downs”).
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more cautious in Q&A on forecasting; President, CEO & Director Turner emphasized discipline and uncertainty, stating, “we don't give guidance,” while Treasurer Copeland repeatedly framed impacts as limited (e.g., “shouldn't be that impactful”).
  • Compared with Q4 2025, management language stayed consistent on refusing payoff guidance, but shifted to more explicit expense build commentary in Q1 2026 via the quantified IT project range; analysts remained focused on margin and expenses across both quarters.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 2026 emphasized loan growth driven by lighter repayments, while Q4 2025 emphasized that payoffs had been elevated and that “loan growth may remain challenging.”
  • Expense discussion moved from Q4’s expectation of “an uptick” from resets to Q1’s more specific project-driven step-up, with President, CEO & Director Turner quantifying potential monthly expense adds over “3 to 6 quarters.”
  • Margin commentary evolved from Q4’s view that there likely wouldn’t be “a whole lot different” from that quarter to Q1’s framing that modest rate moves “isn't going to move the needle too much,” reflecting continued focus on funding repricing and balance.

Risks and Concerns

  • President, CEO & Director Turner cited “a continuing competitive operating environment,” and said management is “mindful of the volatility and the macroeconomic challenges affecting our borrowers,” including “isolated examples of slower lease ups on projects.”
  • Funding competition remained a recurring theme; President, CEO & Director Turner said “deposit markets remain competitive across both core and broker channels,” and management described active funding-mix management “with a focus on cost, duration and flexibility.”
  • Execution risk on deferred projects was highlighted; Treasurer Copeland said deferred initiatives are expected to start “throughout the remainder of 2026,” and he added, “we do expect noninterest expense levels will increase a bit throughout the year.”

Final Takeaway

Management characterized Q1 2026 as a solid start with strong credit metrics, stable deposits, and a net interest margin described as resilient, while repeatedly emphasizing that loan payoffs remain the key uncertainty and the reason it does not provide loan-growth guidance. In Q&A, the most concrete forward-looking detail was the planned technology and security investment cycle, which management said could lift expenses by $200,000 to $250,000 per month as projects come online over the next 3 to 6 quarters, alongside continued capital deployment via buybacks and the regular $0.43 quarterly dividend.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Exxon pulls offer to sell two initial Golden Pass LNG cargoes - Reuters
data.description:
Exxon Mobil (XOM) has withdrawn an offer to sell two initial cargoes of liquefied natural gas from its Golden Pass export plant in Texas that has been in the process of starting up operations, Reuters reported Thursday.

The report did not provide a reason for the withdrawal, but Golden Pass has been running at around a third of its capacity since it started ⁠production last month, taking in ~287M cf/day of natural gas ​on Thursday to be liquefied for export, according to LSEG data; the plant’s first train has a nameplate capacity of 800M cf/day.

Golden Pass had been expected to load ​its first cargo for export later this month, but the project has faced delays and cost overruns since construction began ⁠in 2019, ​including the bankruptcy of its original lead contractor.

QatarEnergy holds a 70% stake in the $10B project, while Exxon (XOM) owns the remaining 30%.

data.title: NiSource rises after signing data center deals with Google, Amazon
data.description:
NiSource (NI) up 2.4% post-market Thursday after unveiling energy infrastructure agreements with units of Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) and Amazon (AMZN) to support data center development in Indiana.

NiSource (NI) said it signed a new long-term energy supply agreement with a unit of Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) to ​support the development and operation of a large-scale data center in northern Indiana beginning in summer 2026, and it expanded an agreement with Amazon (AMZN) to speed up power delivery to its sites and bring forward bill credits for residential customers.

Capacity and energy for the agreements will be supplied utilizing a GenCo-owned pooled portfolio of dedicated electric generation assets for large-load customers while shielding existing customers from added costs and delivering system-wide savings.

NiSource (NI) said the GenCo model aims to ensure existing customers benefit from new ​large users, with savings estimated at ~$1.25B, or $90-$115 annually per household.

data.title: Centurion plans $300,000 private placement
data.description:
• Centurion Minerals (CTN:CA) on Thursday said it plans to raise $300,000 through a non-brokered private placement.
  • The offering will consist of units priced at $0.05 each, with each unit comprising one common share and one warrant exercisable at $0.10 for three years.

  • The company said proceeds will be used for exploration, working capital, and general corporate purposes, with the financing subject to TSX Venture Exchange approval.

  • TMXXF closed +2.54% at $39.12.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Sl Green targets 95% year-end same-store occupancy while advancing a $2.5B disposition plan
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: SL Green Realty Corp. (SLG) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "We had the single biggest first quarter in the 28-year history of this company" as SL Green "signed 51 leases totaling 930,000 square feet" with "a mark-to-market that was 16% higher than the previously fully escalated rents on the same spaces," said Chairman & CEO Marc Holliday.
  • "The vacancy rate for trophy buildings dropped again to 3.4%" and Holliday said "there are 0 new space deliveries anticipated for the next 3 years," adding that new projects "343 Madison and 625 Madison" are "not expected to complete until sometime around '29 or 2030."
  • On development, Holliday said SL Green is moving "at a very rapid pace" at 346 Madison, with "100% schematic design on May 1" and expectations to file into ULURP "by the end of this year," with "financial details" planned after pricing and trade feedback.
  • On 750 Third Avenue, Holliday said SL Green has "an agreement with our final remaining tenant for full vacant possession," is "tracking on or below budget by successfully navigating tariffs and inflation," and "hope[s] to finalize our arrangements for debt and equity capital" in coming months.
  • "More to come in the ensuing months as we progress our way through the $2.5 billion disposition plan," Holliday said, also noting SL Green "put out $226 million" via the debt fund since the last call, bringing "total committed to about $567 million" out of a "total $1.3 billion fund."
  • "We announced the promotion of Harrison Sitomer to President and CIO," Holliday said, calling it "a big step towards identifying, growing and supporting the next generation of leaders here."
  • "We increased...our year-end same-store occupancy target from 94.8% to 95%," said CFO Matthew Diliberto.

Outlook

  • "We are forecasting a big boost and shot in the arm...which bodes well for Summit," Holliday said, citing June/July catalysts including "FIFA World Cup" and "the nation's 250th birthday celebrations."
  • Diliberto said SL Green remains aligned with full-year expectations: "As we look out over the balance of the year, we are headed right to the midpoint of our guidance range as well," and added he felt "comfortable about where we are in the guidance range, biased to the higher end versus the lower end of our range."
  • On earnings cadence, Diliberto said quarterly results can be "choppy" due to fee income: "A lot of those fees come in big chunks...Success fees out of special servicing, fees from transactions," and he referenced "DPOs that we still have in our projections for the balance of the year."
  • Compared with last quarter, SL Green’s occupancy target was raised ("from 94.8% to 95%"), and management reiterated longer-dated cash-flow framing, with Holliday stating, "by the time we get to 228, we expect our FAD to be in line with our dividend...and then hopefully and more."

Financial results

  • SL Green reported record leasing volume and spreads in the quarter: "51 leases totaling 930,000 square feet" with "mark-to-market" of "16%," Holliday said.
  • Diliberto disclosed portfolio occupancy metrics: leased occupancy at "94.4%" and economic occupancy at "85.9%," with an end-of-year economic occupancy objective of "89%."
  • Diliberto highlighted same-store cash NOI performance: "That 2.6% positive was 300 basis points higher than what we expected for the first quarter," and said it contributed to his view that results were "biased to the midpoint or higher" of guidance.
  • On dividends, Diliberto said the reset was driven primarily by taxable income and set at "$2.47," adding it "does allow us to retain almost $50 million of incremental capital" for uses including "DPOs, maybe buybacks."

Q&A

  • Steve Sakwa, Evercore ISI: Asked about the "900,000" square-foot pipeline mix and tenant behavior; Chairman & CEO Holliday said the pipeline reflects what’s "available in our portfolio" and cited a goal that "2/3 of our buildings are projected to be...98% or better by the end of this year," while EVP Steven Durels added "30% of that pipeline is leases out" and said "free rent is clearly starting to come down."
  • John Kim, BMO: Asked about upside to occupancy and spreads; CFO Diliberto said, "we increased...our year-end same-store occupancy target...to 95%" and on spreads added, "we typically don't revisit leasing objectives after just 3 months."
  • Alexander Goldfarb, Piper Sandler: Asked whether private credit stress could spread; Chairman & CEO Holliday said, "we're just not seeing it" and referenced the One Madison financing with "44 distinct investors" and "certain classes 7x oversubscribed."
  • Nicholas Yulico, Scotiabank: Asked about no new supply dynamics and FFO cadence; EVP Durels said tenants are doing "early renewals" and cited 1185 Sixth Avenue rents "into the mid-80s to mid-90s a square foot," while CFO Diliberto said Q1 was "not even $0.01 off" internal expectations and noted fee income lumpiness.
  • Anthony Paolone, JPMorgan: Asked about the leased vs. economic occupancy gap and market liquidity; CFO Diliberto said a normalized gap could be "200 basis points" and described dispositions progress, saying, "we have 11 transactions in the business plan" and now "6" dispositions being worked on.
  • Seth Bergey, Citi: Asked about Summit pricing/upsells and buyer profiles/AI; Chairman & CEO Holliday said "Ascent" is the "only upsell," noted "tourism...is off a bit" but said April improved and he expects Summit "ahead of our ambitious targets," while President & CIO Sitomer said investors are "very optimistic" given leasing data.
  • Ronald Kamdem, Morgan Stanley: Pressed on dividend cut rationale and FAD; CFO Diliberto said, "taxable income is what above all else, drives the dividend," and Chairman & CEO Holliday said, "we are leasing the hell out of this portfolio" while acknowledging "there will be a cost to that" in leasing capital.
  • Blaine Heck, Wells Fargo: Asked how sale proceeds align with the $2.5B plan and views on the new mayor; President & CIO Sitomer said proceeds from targeted first-half sales are "approximately half," and Holliday said it is "too early to assess" the administration but added he sees tenants "expanding rather than contracting."
  • Peter Abramowitz, Deutsche Bank: Asked what could drive the high end of guidance and budget-gap solutions; CFO Diliberto cited NOI outperformance and said the fee business is "very, very high margin," while Holliday said the city will reach a balanced budget through "some revenue reforecast and some expense reductions."
  • Vikram Malhotra, Mizuho: Pressed for cash-flow guardrails and Summit expansion timing; Holliday said, "our projections are unchanged" and reiterated the company expects by "2028" to "cover that dividend and more" with FAD, and he said, "We expect to open Paris in summer of '27."
  • Brendan Lynch, Barclays: Asked about lowering cost of debt and buyback triggers; CFO Diliberto said SL Green has "about $3 billion or so left" of a "$7 billion financing plan" for 2026 and expects to benefit as the curve "come[s] down," while Holliday said, "the stock is terribly mispriced" and that with incremental liquidity, "share repurchases are going to get the first and hardest look."

Sentiment analysis

  • Slightly negative analyst tone centered on cash flow, dividends, and visibility, with repeated probing such as Kamdem asking "why not cut the dividend even more" and Malhotra requesting "guardrails" on when leasing gains translate to cash flow.
  • Positive management tone in prepared remarks emphasized record leasing and supply scarcity, with Holliday stating, "I think this was a great quarter" and later adding, "I've never seen a market as good as this one."
  • Management responses in Q&A remained confident and occasionally corrective on framing, including Diliberto saying quarters can be "choppy" and Holliday pushing back on cash-flow timing with "we'll get there probably with that kind of guidance in '27, but not today."
  • Compared with the prior quarter, management maintained a similar emphasis on capital markets access and New York demand, while the analyst focus shifted more directly to dividend mechanics and FAD timing following the dividend reset to $2.47.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Leasing strength accelerated in Q1, with Holliday calling it the company’s "single biggest first quarter" and reporting 930,000 square feet, while Q4 highlighted "almost 800,000" square feet leased in the quarter and a "pipeline of more than 1 million square feet."
  • Guidance language tightened on occupancy as Diliberto raised the year-end same-store occupancy target to "95%" from "94.8%," whereas Q4 framed 94.8% as the year-end objective.
  • Leadership update was a clear change: Q4 listed Holliday as "Interim President," while Q1 emphasized "the promotion of Harrison Sitomer to President and CIO."
  • Analyst questioning remained heavily oriented to cash flow and dividends in both quarters, but Q1 included more direct buyback discussion, with Holliday stating repurchases would get "the first and hardest look" after business-plan priorities.

Risks and concerns

  • Summit sensitivity to weather and tourism was highlighted, with Diliberto noting "it was a tough weather quarter" and Holliday adding "tourism in the city is off a bit."
  • Cash flow and leasing-capital drag remained a central concern, with management acknowledging continued spend: Holliday said there will be "spend years for the balance of this year and next" as SL Green targets "96%, 97%, 98% occupancy."
  • Development execution and financing remain milestones, with Holliday stating 346 Madison financial details will follow once the project is priced and 750 Third Avenue capital arrangements are expected to be finalized in coming months.

Final takeaway

Management described Q1 as a record leasing quarter, tied to a prime-office supply squeeze and improving net effective rent dynamics, while reaffirming a full-year path toward the midpoint to upper end of guidance. The company raised its year-end same-store occupancy target to 95%, reiterated a $2.5B disposition plan, and outlined major near-term catalysts and milestones across developments (346 Madison and 750 Third) and Summit, while repeatedly positioning dividend policy around taxable income and targeting improved FAD coverage by 2028.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: InvenTrust Properties raises $250M via private placement of senior notes
data.description:
• InvenTrust Properties (IVT) announced on Thursday that it has raised $250M through a private placement of senior unsecured notes, split into three series: Series A ($50M at 5.09% due 2029), Series B ($100M at 5.32% due 2031), and Series C ($100M at 5.60% due 2033).
  • The notes are expected to mature in about 5.4 years and pay a fixed interest rate of ~5.44% on average.
  • No subsidiary guarantees are expected at issuance; guarantees may apply only if required under existing primary credit facilities.
  • The net proceeds from the offering will be used for general corporate purposes, including debt repayment.
  • The notes are expected to be issued on June 29, 2026.
  • The stock traded higher to $32.10 in the after-hours trading session.
  • Source: Press Release
